inputs.question: What is the current evidence on pre-exercise reactive (rebound) hypoglycaemia in sport nutrition? Specifically: mechanism, does it impair endurance performance, does carbohydrate timing/glycemic-index/dose modify risk, and what are practical avoidance strategies for athletes?

output.text:
Pre-exercise reactive hypoglycemia occurs in some but not all athletes, typically does not impair performance, and can be minimized through strategic timing and carbohydrate selection.

Mechanism

Reactive hypoglycemia after pre-exercise carbohydrate intake results from an exaggerated insulin response that persists into the early exercise period. When carbohydrate is consumed 30-90 minutes before exercise (with peak risk around 60 minutes), the resulting insulin surge suppresses hepatic glucose output while simultaneously increasing glucose uptake. At exercise onset, muscle glucose uptake increases further, but hepatic glucose production may not compensate adequately if insulin levels remain elevated, leading to transient hypoglycemia (typically <70 mg/dL or <4.0 mmol/L) within the first 15-30 minutes of exercise.[[[$$$Kondo S, Tanisawa K, Suzuki K, Terada S, Higuchi M. Preexercise Carbohydrate Ingestion and Transient Hypoglycemia: Fasting Versus Feeding. Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise. 2019;51(1):168-173. doi:10.1249/MSS.0000000000001773.$$$]!!![$$$Zignoli A, Fontana FY, Lipman DJ, et al. Association Between Pre-Exercise Food Ingestion Timing and Reactive Hypoglycemia: Insights From a Large Database of Continuous Glucose Monitoring Data. European Journal of Sport Science. 2023;23(12):2340-2348. doi:10.1080/17461391.2023.2233468.$$$]]] Individual susceptibility varies considerably. Athletes with higher insulin sensitivity are more prone to reactive hypoglycemia, particularly in the fasted state.[[[$$$Kondo S, Tanisawa K, Suzuki K, Terada S, Higuchi M. Preexercise Carbohydrate Ingestion and Transient Hypoglycemia: Fasting Versus Feeding. Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise. 2019;51(1):168-173. doi:10.1249/MSS.0000000000001773.$$$]!!![$$$Kuipers H, Fransen EJ, Keizer HA. Pre-Exercise Ingestion of Carbohydrate and Transient Hypoglycemia During Exercise. International Journal of Sports Medicine. 1999;20(4):227-31. doi:10.1055/s-2007-971122.$$$]]] In one study, approximately 30% of trained cyclists developed transient hypoglycemia after consuming 50g glucose 30 minutes before exercise, with those experiencing hypoglycemia demonstrating higher insulin sensitivity and lower sympathetic nervous system activity (lower norepinephrine responses).[[[$$$Kuipers H, Fransen EJ, Keizer HA. Pre-Exercise Ingestion of Carbohydrate and Transient Hypoglycemia During Exercise. International Journal of Sports Medicine. 1999;20(4):227-31. doi:10.1055/s-2007-971122.$$$]]] Large-scale continuous glucose monitoring data confirms that reactive hypoglycemia occurs in approximately 8% of pre-exercise feeding events overall, though some individuals experience it in >20% of their sessions.[[[$$$Zignoli A, Fontana FY, Lipman DJ, et al. Association Between Pre-Exercise Food Ingestion Timing and Reactive Hypoglycemia: Insights From a Large Database of Continuous Glucose Monitoring Data. European Journal of Sport Science. 2023;23(12):2340-2348. doi:10.1080/17461391.2023.2233468.$$$]]]

Performance Impact

Despite metabolic perturbations, reactive hypoglycemia rarely impairs endurance performance. A comprehensive analysis of available studies concluded that "the risk of reduced performance is minimal as almost all studies point towards unaltered or even improved performance".[[[$$$Jeukendrup AE, Killer SC. The Myths Surrounding Pre-Exercise Carbohydrate Feeding. Annals of Nutrition & Metabolism. 2010;57 Suppl 2:18-25. doi:10.1159/000322698.$$$]]] This finding is consistent even when blood glucose transiently drops below hypoglycemic thresholds.[[[$$$Kondo S, Tanisawa K, Suzuki K, Terada S, Higuchi M. Preexercise Carbohydrate Ingestion and Transient Hypoglycemia: Fasting Versus Feeding. Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise. 2019;51(1):168-173. doi:10.1249/MSS.0000000000001773.$$$]!!![$$$Jeukendrup AE, Killer SC. The Myths Surrounding Pre-Exercise Carbohydrate Feeding. Annals of Nutrition & Metabolism. 2010;57 Suppl 2:18-25. doi:10.1159/000322698.$$$]]] Importantly, athletes may experience symptoms resembling hypoglycemia (lightheadedness, weakness) even when glucose levels remain normal, and these symptoms do not correlate with actual performance decrements.[[[$$$Jeukendrup AE, Killer SC. The Myths Surrounding Pre-Exercise Carbohydrate Feeding. Annals of Nutrition & Metabolism. 2010;57 Suppl 2:18-25. doi:10.1159/000322698.$$$]]] A 2026 review emphasizes that while exercise-induced hypoglycemia (EIH) correlates with fatigue in prolonged exercise (>2-3 hours), the primary benefit of carbohydrate ingestion is preventing EIH rather than sparing muscle glycogen.[[[$$$Noakes TD, Prins PJ, Buga A, et al. Carbohydrate Ingestion on Exercise Metabolism and Physical Performance. Endocrine Reviews. 2026;47(2):191-243. doi:10.1210/endrev/bnaf038.$$$]]]

Carbohydrate Timing, Glycemic Index, and Dose

Timing: The highest risk window for reactive hypoglycemia is 30-90 minutes pre-exercise, peaking at 60 minutes.[[[$$$Zignoli A, Fontana FY, Lipman DJ, et al. Association Between Pre-Exercise Food Ingestion Timing and Reactive Hypoglycemia: Insights From a Large Database of Continuous Glucose Monitoring Data. European Journal of Sport Science. 2023;23(12):2340-2348. doi:10.1080/17461391.2023.2233468.$$$]]] Consuming carbohydrate either well before this window (>90-120 minutes) or very close to exercise onset (<15 minutes) reduces risk. Feeding closer to exercise initiation allows less time for insulin to peak before muscle glucose uptake increases. Glycemic Index: Low glycemic index (LGI) carbohydrates produce smaller insulin responses and more stable glucose levels compared to high glycemic index (HGI) options. Studies demonstrate that LGI pre-exercise meals reduce the magnitude of blood glucose decline during exercise.[[[$$$Ferland A, Brassard P, Lemieux S, et al. Impact of High-Fat /Low-Carbohydrate, High-, Low-Glycaemic Index or Low-Caloric Meals on Glucose Regulation During Aerobic Exercise in Type 2 Diabetes. Diabetic Medicine : A Journal of the British Diabetic Association. 2009;26(6):589-95. doi:10.1111/j.1464-5491.2009.02734.x.$$$]!!![$$$Bennett CB, Chilibeck PD, Barss T, et al. Metabolism and Performance During Extended High-Intensity Intermittent Exercise After Consumption of Low- And High-Glycaemic Index Pre-Exercise Meals. The British Journal of Nutrition. 2012;108 Suppl 1:S81-90. doi:10.1017/S0007114512000840.$$$]!!![$$$Stevenson EJ, Williams C, Mash LE, Phillips B, Nute ML. Influence of High-Carbohydrate Mixed Meals With Different Glycemic Indexes on Substrate Utilization During Subsequent Exercise in Women. The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition. 2006;84(2):354-60. doi:10.1093/ajcn/84.1.354.$$$]!!![$$$Febbraio MA, Keenan J, Angus DJ, Campbell SE, Garnham AP. Preexercise Carbohydrate Ingestion, Glucose Kinetics, and Muscle Glycogen Use: Effect of the Glycemic Index. Journal of Applied Physiology (Bethesda, Md. : 1985). 2000;89(5):1845-51. doi:10.1152/jappl.2000.89.5.1845.$$$]]] For example, one study found that at exercise onset, blood glucose fell significantly in the HGI trial but remained stable with LGI feeding, with glucose levels lower at 15 and 30 minutes of exercise after HGI consumption.[[[$$$Febbraio MA, Keenan J, Angus DJ, Campbell SE, Garnham AP. Preexercise Carbohydrate Ingestion, Glucose Kinetics, and Muscle Glycogen Use: Effect of the Glycemic Index. Journal of Applied Physiology (Bethesda, Md. : 1985). 2000;89(5):1845-51. doi:10.1152/jappl.2000.89.5.1845.$$$]]] However, the American College of Sports Medicine notes that LGI strategies have "not been found to provide a universal benefit to performance even when the metabolic perturbations of pre-exercise carbohydrate intake are attenuated".[[[$$$Thomas DT, Erdman KA, Burke LM. American College of Sports Medicine Joint Position Statement. Nutrition and Athletic Performance. Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise. 2016;48(3):543-68. doi:10.1249/MSS.0000000000000852.$$$]!!![$$$Thomas DT, Erdman KA, Burke LM. American College of Sports Medicine Joint Position Statement. Nutrition and Athletic Performance. Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise. 2016;48(3):543-68. doi:10.1249/MSS.0000000000000852.$$$]]] Dose: Smaller carbohydrate doses appear to increase risk. In one study, 50g of glucose after a 4-hour fast was sufficient to induce transient hypoglycemia in approximately one-third of cyclists.[[[$$$Kuipers H, Fransen EJ, Keizer HA. Pre-Exercise Ingestion of Carbohydrate and Transient Hypoglycemia During Exercise. International Journal of Sports Medicine. 1999;20(4):227-31. doi:10.1055/s-2007-971122.$$$]]] The American College of Sports Medicine recommends at least 1 g/kg body weight in pre-event meals to compensate for increased carbohydrate oxidation.[[[$$$Thomas DT, Erdman KA, Burke LM. American College of Sports Medicine Joint Position Statement. Nutrition and Athletic Performance. Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise. 2016;48(3):543-68. doi:10.1249/MSS.0000000000000852.$$$]!!![$$$Thomas DT, Erdman KA, Burke LM. American College of Sports Medicine Joint Position Statement. Nutrition and Athletic Performance. Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise. 2016;48(3):543-68. doi:10.1249/MSS.0000000000000852.$$$]]] Larger doses may provide sufficient substrate to offset the insulin-mediated suppression of hepatic glucose output.

Practical Avoidance Strategies

Evidence-based strategies to minimize reactive hypoglycemia include: Timing modifications: - Consume pre-exercise meals >90-120 minutes before exercise to allow insulin levels to normalize, or - Feed within 15 minutes of exercise onset to minimize the insulin peak-exercise overlap window[[[$$$Zignoli A, Fontana FY, Lipman DJ, et al. Association Between Pre-Exercise Food Ingestion Timing and Reactive Hypoglycemia: Insights From a Large Database of Continuous Glucose Monitoring Data. European Journal of Sport Science. 2023;23(12):2340-2348. doi:10.1080/17461391.2023.2233468.$$$]]] - Avoid the 30-90 minute pre-exercise window in susceptible individuals[[[$$$Zignoli A, Fontana FY, Lipman DJ, et al. Association Between Pre-Exercise Food Ingestion Timing and Reactive Hypoglycemia: Insights From a Large Database of Continuous Glucose Monitoring Data. European Journal of Sport Science. 2023;23(12):2340-2348. doi:10.1080/17461391.2023.2233468.$$$]]] Carbohydrate selection: - Choose low glycemic index carbohydrates (e.g., oats, lentils, isomaltulose) for meals consumed 2-3 hours pre-exercise to reduce insulin spikes and provide sustained glucose release[[[$$$Riddell MC, Gallen IW, Smart CE, et al. Exercise Management in Type 1 Diabetes: A Consensus Statement. The Lancet. Diabetes & Endocrinology. 2017;5(5):377-390. doi:10.1016/S2213-8587(17)30014-1.$$$]!!![$$$Hamilton RA, Xia R, Nicholas C, et al. Glycaemic Impact of Low- And High-Glycaemic Index Carbohydrate Diets in Ultra-Endurance Athletes: Insights From Continuous Glucose Monitoring. European Journal of Sport Science. 2025;25(12):e70092. doi:10.1002/ejsc.70092.$$$]!!![$$$Ferland A, Brassard P, Lemieux S, et al. Impact of High-Fat /Low-Carbohydrate, High-, Low-Glycaemic Index or Low-Caloric Meals on Glucose Regulation During Aerobic Exercise in Type 2 Diabetes. Diabetic Medicine : A Journal of the British Diabetic Association. 2009;26(6):589-95. doi:10.1111/j.1464-5491.2009.02734.x.$$$]!!![$$$Bennett CB, Chilibeck PD, Barss T, et al. Metabolism and Performance During Extended High-Intensity Intermittent Exercise After Consumption of Low- And High-Glycaemic Index Pre-Exercise Meals. The British Journal of Nutrition. 2012;108 Suppl 1:S81-90. doi:10.1017/S0007114512000840.$$$]]] - Consider mixed macronutrient meals including protein and fat to blunt glycemic and insulin responses[[[$$$Thomas DT, Erdman KA, Burke LM. American College of Sports Medicine Joint Position Statement. Nutrition and Athletic Performance. Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise. 2016;48(3):543-68. doi:10.1249/MSS.0000000000000852.$$$]!!![$$$Thomas DT, Erdman KA, Burke LM. American College of Sports Medicine Joint Position Statement. Nutrition and Athletic Performance. Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise. 2016;48(3):543-68. doi:10.1249/MSS.0000000000000852.$$$]]] Dose optimization: - Ensure adequate carbohydrate intake (≥1 g/kg body weight) in pre-exercise meals to compensate for increased oxidation[[[$$$Thomas DT, Erdman KA, Burke LM. American College of Sports Medicine Joint Position Statement. Nutrition and Athletic Performance. Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise. 2016;48(3):543-68. doi:10.1249/MSS.0000000000000852.$$$]!!![$$$Thomas DT, Erdman KA, Burke LM. American College of Sports Medicine Joint Position Statement. Nutrition and Athletic Performance. Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise. 2016;48(3):543-68. doi:10.1249/MSS.0000000000000852.$$$]]] - Smaller doses (<50g) may increase susceptibility in sensitive individuals[[[$$$Kuipers H, Fransen EJ, Keizer HA. Pre-Exercise Ingestion of Carbohydrate and Transient Hypoglycemia During Exercise. International Journal of Sports Medicine. 1999;20(4):227-31. doi:10.1055/s-2007-971122.$$$]]] During-exercise feeding: - Consume carbohydrate during exercise (30-60 g/hour for endurance events), which dampens any metabolic effects of pre-exercise feeding and prevents hypoglycemia[[[$$$Thomas DT, Erdman KA, Burke LM. American College of Sports Medicine Joint Position Statement. Nutrition and Athletic Performance. Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise. 2016;48(3):543-68. doi:10.1249/MSS.0000000000000852.$$$]!!![$$$Thomas DT, Erdman KA, Burke LM. American College of Sports Medicine Joint Position Statement. Nutrition and Athletic Performance. Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise. 2016;48(3):543-68. doi:10.1249/MSS.0000000000000852.$$$]!!![$$$Thomas DT, Erdman KA, Burke LM. American College of Sports Medicine Joint Position Statement. Nutrition and Athletic Performance. Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise. 2016;48(3):543-68. doi:10.1249/MSS.0000000000000852.$$$]]] - This strategy is particularly important for events >60 minutes[[[$$$Thomas DT, Erdman KA, Burke LM. American College of Sports Medicine Joint Position Statement. Nutrition and Athletic Performance. Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise. 2016;48(3):543-68. doi:10.1249/MSS.0000000000000852.$$$]]] Warm-up strategies: - Include brief high-intensity efforts in the warm-up to stimulate hepatic gluconeogenesis and counterregulatory hormone release[[[$$$Thomas DT, Erdman KA, Burke LM. American College of Sports Medicine Joint Position Statement. Nutrition and Athletic Performance. Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise. 2016;48(3):543-68. doi:10.1249/MSS.0000000000000852.$$$]!!![$$$Thomas DT, Erdman KA, Burke LM. American College of Sports Medicine Joint Position Statement. Nutrition and Athletic Performance. Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise. 2016;48(3):543-68. doi:10.1249/MSS.0000000000000852.$$$]]] - Short sprints or high-intensity intervals before aerobic exercise can attenuate hypoglycemic responses[[[$$$Trojian T, Colberg S, Harris G, et al. American Medical Society for Sports Medicine Position Statement on the Care of the Athlete and Athletic Person With Diabetes. Clinical Journal of Sport Medicine : Official Journal of the Canadian Academy of Sport Medicine. 2022;32(1):8-20. doi:10.1097/JSM.0000000000000906.$$$]]] Individual assessment: - Recognize that responses are highly individual[[[$$$Kondo S, Tanisawa K, Suzuki K, Terada S, Higuchi M. Preexercise Carbohydrate Ingestion and Transient Hypoglycemia: Fasting Versus Feeding. Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise. 2019;51(1):168-173. doi:10.1249/MSS.0000000000001773.$$$]!!![$$$Jeukendrup AE, Killer SC. The Myths Surrounding Pre-Exercise Carbohydrate Feeding. Annals of Nutrition & Metabolism. 2010;57 Suppl 2:18-25. doi:10.1159/000322698.$$$]!!![$$$Kuipers H, Fransen EJ, Keizer HA. structured_article.raw_text:
**Pre-exercise reactive hypoglycemia occurs in some but not all athletes, typically does not impair performance, and can be minimized through strategic timing and carbohydrate selection.**

## Mechanism

Reactive hypoglycemia after pre-exercise carbohydrate intake results from an **exaggerated insulin response** that persists into the early exercise period. When carbohydrate is consumed 30-90 minutes before exercise (with peak risk around 60 minutes), the resulting insulin surge suppresses hepatic glucose output while simultaneously increasing glucose uptake. At exercise onset, muscle glucose uptake increases further, but hepatic glucose production may not compensate adequately if insulin levels remain elevated, leading to transient hypoglycemia (typically <70 mg/dL or <4.0 mmol/L) within the first 15-30 minutes of exercise[1][2].

Individual susceptibility varies considerably. Athletes with **higher insulin sensitivity** are more prone to reactive hypoglycemia, particularly in the fasted state[1][5]. In one study, approximately 30% of trained cyclists developed transient hypoglycemia after consuming 50g glucose 30 minutes before exercise, with those experiencing hypoglycemia demonstrating higher insulin sensitivity and lower sympathetic nervous system activity (lower norepinephrine responses)[5]. Large-scale continuous glucose monitoring data confirms that reactive hypoglycemia occurs in approximately 8% of pre-exercise feeding events overall, though some individuals experience it in >20% of their sessions[2].

## Performance Impact

Despite metabolic perturbations, **reactive hypoglycemia rarely impairs endurance performance**. A comprehensive analysis of available studies concluded that "the risk of reduced performance is minimal as almost all studies point towards unaltered or even improved performance"[4]. This finding is consistent even when blood glucose transiently drops below hypoglycemic thresholds[1][4].

Importantly, athletes may experience **symptoms resembling hypoglycemia** (lightheadedness, weakness) even when glucose levels remain normal, and these symptoms do not correlate with actual performance decrements[4]. A 2026 review emphasizes that while exercise-induced hypoglycemia (EIH) correlates with fatigue in prolonged exercise (>2-3 hours), the primary benefit of carbohydrate ingestion is preventing EIH rather than sparing muscle glycogen[3].

## Carbohydrate Timing, Glycemic Index, and Dose

**Timing:** The highest risk window for reactive hypoglycemia is **30-90 minutes pre-exercise, peaking at 60 minutes**[2]. Consuming carbohydrate either well before this window (>90-120 minutes) or very close to exercise onset (<15 minutes) reduces risk. Feeding closer to exercise initiation allows less time for insulin to peak before muscle glucose uptake increases.

**Glycemic Index:** Low glycemic index (LGI) carbohydrates produce **smaller insulin responses and more stable glucose levels** compared to high glycemic index (HGI) options. Studies demonstrate that LGI pre-exercise meals reduce the magnitude of blood glucose decline during exercise[23][24][25][26]. For example, one study found that at exercise onset, blood glucose fell significantly in the HGI trial but remained stable with LGI feeding, with glucose levels lower at 15 and 30 minutes of exercise after HGI consumption[26]. However, the American College of Sports Medicine notes that LGI strategies have "not been found to provide a universal benefit to performance even when the metabolic perturbations of pre-exercise carbohydrate intake are attenuated"[10][14].

**Dose:** Smaller carbohydrate doses appear to increase risk. In one study, 50g of glucose after a 4-hour fast was sufficient to induce transient hypoglycemia in approximately one-third of cyclists[5]. The American College of Sports Medicine recommends **at least 1 g/kg body weight** in pre-event meals to compensate for increased carbohydrate oxidation[10][14]. Larger doses may provide sufficient substrate to offset the insulin-mediated suppression of hepatic glucose output.

## Practical Avoidance Strategies

Evidence-based strategies to minimize reactive hypoglycemia include:

**Timing modifications:**
- Consume pre-exercise meals **>90-120 minutes before exercise** to allow insulin levels to normalize, or
- Feed **within 15 minutes of exercise onset** to minimize the insulin peak-exercise overlap window[2]
- Avoid the 30-90 minute pre-exercise window in susceptible individuals[2]

**Carbohydrate selection:**
- Choose **low glycemic index carbohydrates** (e.g., oats, lentils, isomaltulose) for meals consumed 2-3 hours pre-exercise to reduce insulin spikes and provide sustained glucose release[20][21][23][24]
- Consider **mixed macronutrient meals** including protein and fat to blunt glycemic and insulin responses[10][14]

**Dose optimization:**
- Ensure adequate carbohydrate intake (**≥1 g/kg body weight**) in pre-exercise meals to compensate for increased oxidation[10][14]
- Smaller doses (<50g) may increase susceptibility in sensitive individuals[5]

**During-exercise feeding:**
- **Consume carbohydrate during exercise** (30-60 g/hour for endurance events), which dampens any metabolic effects of pre-exercise feeding and prevents hypoglycemia[10][14][32]
- This strategy is particularly important for events >60 minutes[10]

**Warm-up strategies:**
- Include **brief high-intensity efforts** in the warm-up to stimulate hepatic gluconeogenesis and counterregulatory hormone release[10][14]
- Short sprints or high-intensity intervals before aerobic exercise can attenuate hypoglycemic responses[6]

**Individual assessment:**
- Recognize that responses are **highly individual**[1][4][5]
- Athletes prone to reactive hypoglycemia should experiment with timing and food choices during training
- Continuous glucose monitoring can identify personal patterns and susceptibility[2]

The American College of Sports Medicine emphasizes that "consumption of carbohydrate during exercise dampens any effects of pre-exercise carbohydrate intake on metabolism and performance," making during-exercise feeding the most reliable strategy for preventing both reactive and exercise-induced hypoglycemia[10][14].

Would you like to explore specific carbohydrate feeding protocols for different endurance event durations, or strategies for athletes who consistently experience reactive hypoglycemia despite these interventions?

citation_detail.title: The Myths Surrounding Pre-Exercise Carbohydrate Feeding
citation_detail.journal_name: Annals of Nutrition & Metabolism
citation_detail.authors_string: Jeukendrup AE, Killer SC.
citation_detail.publication_info_string: Annals of Nutrition & Metabolism. 2010;57 Suppl 2:18-25. doi:10.1159/000322698.

articlespan_set.text: Importantly, athletes may experience
symptoms resembling hypoglycemia
(lightheadedness, weakness) even when glucose levels remain normal, and these symptoms do not correlate with actual performance decrements.

citations.citation: Jeukendrup AE, Killer SC. The Myths Surrounding Pre-Exercise Carbohydrate Feeding. Annals of Nutrition & Metabolism. 2010;57 Suppl 2:18-25. doi:10.1159/000322698.

citation_detail.title: The Myths Surrounding Pre-Exercise Carbohydrate Feeding
citation_detail.journal_name: Annals of Nutrition & Metabolism
citation_detail.authors_string: Jeukendrup AE, Killer SC.
citation_detail.publication_info_string: Annals of Nutrition & Metabolism. 2010;57 Suppl 2:18-25. doi:10.1159/000322698.

articlespan_set.text: A 2026 review emphasizes that while exercise-induced hypoglycemia (EIH) correlates with fatigue in prolonged exercise (>2-3 hours), the primary benefit of carbohydrate ingestion is preventing EIH rather than sparing muscle glycogen.

citations.citation: Noakes TD, Prins PJ, Buga A, et al. Carbohydrate Ingestion on Exercise Metabolism and Physical Performance. Endocrine Reviews. 2026;47(2):191-243. doi:10.1210/endrev/bnaf038.

citation_detail.title: Carbohydrate Ingestion on Exercise Metabolism and Physical Performance
citation_detail.journal_name: Endocrine Reviews
citation_detail.authors_string: Noakes TD, Prins PJ, Buga A, et al.
citation_detail.publication_info_string: Endocrine Reviews. 2026;47(2):191-243. doi:10.1210/endrev/bnaf038.

articlespan_set.text: Carbohydrate Timing, Glycemic Index, and Dose
Timing:
The highest risk window for reactive hypoglycemia is
30-90 minutes pre-exercise, peaking at 60 minutes

citations.citation: Zignoli A, Fontana FY, Lipman DJ, et al. Association Between Pre-Exercise Food Ingestion Timing and Reactive Hypoglycemia: Insights From a Large Database of Continuous Glucose Monitoring Data. European Journal of Sport Science. 2023;23(12):2340-2348. doi:10.1080/17461391.2023.2233468.

citation_detail.title: Association Between Pre-Exercise Food Ingestion Timing and Reactive Hypoglycemia: Insights From a Large Database of Continuous Glucose Monitoring Data
citation_detail.journal_name: European Journal of Sport Science
citation_detail.authors_string: Zignoli A, Fontana FY, Lipman DJ, et al.

articlespan_set.text: Consuming carbohydrate either well before this window (>90-120 minutes) or very close to exercise onset (<15 minutes) reduces risk.
Feeding closer to exercise initiation allows less time for insulin to peak before muscle glucose uptake increases.
Glycemic Index:
Low glycemic index (LGI) carbohydrates produce
smaller insulin responses and more stable glucose levels
compared to high glycemic index (HGI) options.
Studies demonstrate that LGI pre-exercise meals reduce the magnitude of blood glucose decline during exercise.

articlespan_set.text: For example, one study found that at exercise onset, blood glucose fell significantly in the HGI trial but remained stable with LGI feeding, with glucose levels lower at 15 and 30 minutes of exercise after HGI consumption.

articlespan_set.text: However, the American College of Sports Medicine notes that LGI strategies have "not been found to provide a universal benefit to performance even when the metabolic perturbations of pre-exercise carbohydrate intake are attenuated".

articlespan_set.text: Dose:
Smaller carbohydrate doses appear to increase risk.
In one study, 50g of glucose after a 4-hour fast was sufficient to induce transient hypoglycemia in approximately one-third of cyclists.

citations.citation: Kuipers H, Fransen EJ, Keizer HA. Pre-Exercise Ingestion of Carbohydrate and Transient Hypoglycemia During Exercise. International Journal of Sports Medicine. 1999;20(4):227-31. doi:10.1055/s-2007-971122.

citation_detail.title: Pre-Exercise Ingestion of Carbohydrate and Transient Hypoglycemia During Exercise
citation_detail.journal_name: International Journal of Sports Medicine
citation_detail.authors_string: Kuipers H, Fransen EJ, Keizer HA.
citation_detail.publication_info_string: International Journal of Sports Medicine. 1999;20(4):227-31. doi:10.1055/s-2007-971122.

articlespan_set.text: The American College of Sports Medicine recommends
at least 1 g/kg body weight
in pre-event meals to compensate for increased carbohydrate oxidation.

citations.citation: Thomas DT, Erdman KA, Burke LM. American College of Sports Medicine Joint Position Statement. Nutrition and Athletic Performance. Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise. 2016;48(3):543-68. doi:10.1249/MSS.0000000000000852.

articlespan_set.text: Larger doses may provide sufficient substrate to offset the insulin-mediated suppression of hepatic glucose output.
Practical Avoidance Strategies
Evidence-based strategies to minimize reactive hypoglycemia include:
Timing modifications:
- Consume pre-exercise meals >90-120 minutes before exercise to allow insulin levels to normalize, or
- Feed within 15 minutes of exercise onset to minimize the insulin peak-exercise overlap window

citation_detail.journal_name: European Journal of Sport Science
citation_detail.authors_string: Zignoli A, Fontana FY, Lipman DJ, et al.

articlespan_set.text: - Avoid the 30-90 minute pre-exercise window in susceptible individuals

citations.citation: Zignoli A, Fontana FY, Lipman DJ, et al. articlespan_set.text: Carbohydrate selection:
- Choose low glycemic index carbohydrates (e.g., oats, lentils, isomaltulose) for meals consumed 2-3 hours pre-exercise to reduce insulin spikes and provide sustained glucose release

citation_detail.authors_string: Thomas DT, Erdman KA, Burke LM.
citation_detail.publication_info_string: Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise. 2016;48(3):543-68. doi:10.1249/MSS.0000000000000852.

articlespan_set.text: Would you like to explore specific carbohydrate feeding protocols for different endurance event durations, or strategies for athletes who consistently experience reactive hypoglycemia despite these interventions?

follow_up_questions: Which populations of athletes are most at risk for pre-exercise reactive hypoglycaemia in practice?
What are the reported side effects of alternative nutritional strategies to prevent rebound hypoglycaemia?
Are there differences in risk or outcomes between endurance and power athletes?
